title: podman 5.5.0 system clock is forward in the containers
body:
### Issue Description

We're using Google Spanner Emulator, which is basically a relational database emulator, the images are `linux/amd64` running on my Mac with Apple silicon (`arm64`).

We're evaluating switching off of Docker and noticed an annoying issue: the clock runs ~150ms forward in the container compared to a system clock. This was never an issue when running a Docker env.



### Steps to reproduce the issue

This is a small test result that I wrote. 

I execute a simple query
```
SELECT current_timestamp()
```
Before and after query execution, I take system clock in my test environment (Java) and then I compare it with what Spanner returned. 

If system clocks are synchronized, this should hold:
```
system_before_query <= returned_timestamp <= system_after_query
```

However, the observed value of the returned timestamp is always ~150ms ahead of `system_after_query` which indicates that clock in the container is ahead.

### Describe the results you received

Here are the logs:
```
# Attempt 1
System (before query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.585881Z 
System (after query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.592035Z
Returned: 2025-05-23T06:33:55.730906000Z, 
Diff is PT0.138871S

# Attempt 2
System (before query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.592381Z 
System (after query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.598286Z
Returned: 2025-05-23T06:33:55.737236000Z, 
Diff is PT0.13895S

# Attempt 3
System (before query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.598525Z 
System (after query): 2025-05-23T06:33:55.604212Z
Returned: 2025-05-23T06:33:55.743216000Z, 
Diff is PT0.139004S
```

### Describe the results you expected

Obviously, clocks should be synchronized. This was never an issue before, but now it causes many test flakes because we rely on time continuity.

### Podman in a container

### Additional information

comments.body: And yes, restarting the VM does not help, it is still out of sync.
@kberezin-nshl can you share a script to make it easier to reproduce the problem?

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

Huh, what a weird issue!

Let's run some tests [1]

Okay so, it seems to be an issue with the Podman machine VM. I can't reproduce the issue in Linux at all, only when testing by exec'ing into a running container on Mac or running commands in the Podman machine itself.

So what's going on here? The Podman machine VM is launched by a project called [vfkit](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit), vfkit has an option for keeping the host and guest time in sync called [--timesync](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/blob/16d5ddd714db62cf1bf21a2cdc1431021dd9a808/doc/usage.md?plain=1#L34-L44), this is needed to sync the guest OS's clock after your Macbook goes to sleep or restarts etc.

Podman doesn't use this feature when spawning the Podman machine but the time is still relatively correct, how is that? Well our guest OS of choice is Fedora CoreOS where we have [Chrony](https://chrony-project.org/) keeping the time correct using NTP and it's scary accurate [2] using multiple time sources and algorithms to reduces jitter and improve offset correction!

So maybe the issue is the Mac clock rather than the VM?

Let's manually sync the Mac clock and see:
```
$ sudo sntp -sS pool.ntp.org
+0.050186 +/- 0.015149 pool.ntp.org 162.159.200.123
```
Wow, now everything looks better!
```
$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 13:04:27:674244000
VM: 13:04:27:840079889
After: 13:04:27:845341000

$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 13:04:32:881833000
VM: 13:04:33:038247500
After: 13:04:33:043617000

$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 13:04:33:559903000
VM: 13:04:33:711672042
After: 13:04:33:716917000
```

But after some time it drifts again though, the host and guest are running different NTP clients with different levels of precision.

You can't even do a hack like disabling chronyd and doing a one time sync with something like:`date=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") && podman machine ssh "sudo timedatectl set-time '$date'"` as both `date` and `timedatectl` can only set the clock to the nearest second...

That vfkit argument I mentioned before, `--timesync`, I wonder if that would solve the issue. It uses `qemu-guest-agent` to set the time and yes, [it can set the guest clock to the nanosecond](https://github.com/qemu/qemu/blob/09be8a511a2e278b45729d7b065d30c68dd699d0/qga/qapi-schema.json#L143-L167)!


So let's get super hacky and manually try it out!:
```
# Install the guest agent
$ podman machine ssh sudo rpm-ostree install qemu-guest-agent -y

# Restart into the new layered root filesystem image
$ podman machine stop
$ podman machine start

# Stop chronyd keeping the time so perfect.
$ podman machine ssh sudo systemctl stop chronyd

# Manually start the qemu-guest-agent listening on a local socket
$ podman machine ssh sudo /usr/bin/qemu-ga -v -m unix-listen -p /var/home/core/socket.sock &

# Check how long it takes to connect to the Podman machine and offset new time by the value
$ time podman machine ssh exit
podman machine ssh exit  0.03s user 0.01s system 24% cpu 0.167 total

# Speak JSON to the socket to command it to set the time to the current Mac time + 167ms ssh connection buffer. 
$ cmd="echo '{\"execute\": \"guest-set-time\", \"arguments\":{\"time\": $(( $(date +%s%N) + 167000000 ))}}' | sudo socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/var/home/core/socket.sock" && \
  podman machine ssh "$cmd"
{"return": {}}
```

Now let's check:
```
$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 14:53:49:110628000
VM: 14:53:49:298367987
After: 14:53:49:317438000
```

Noice 👌 

In conclusion, I think it would be a good idea to disable chronyd and install qemu-guest-agent in our Fedora CoreOS image, then add a small patch to Podman to create the Podman machine VM with the vfkit `--timesync` argument. I think it's more important to be in sync with the guest rather than NTP, even if the guest is slightly inaccurate ;)

Let me know what you think!

[1]
Baseline, running in Linux so no virtualization layer:
```
$ echo -n "Before: " && date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman run --rm fedora date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:24:01:701594479
Container: 11:24:01:818920554
After: 11:24:01:849162803

$ echo -n "Before: " && date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman run --rm fedora date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N

Container: 11:24:02:539847233

Before: 11:24:03:109098625
Container: 11:24:03:232760375
After: 11:24:03:262268790
```

```
$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N

After: 11:32:48:832381000


$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:32:49:470204000
VM: 11:32:49:698146878
After: 11:32:49:655055000

$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "VM: " && podman machine ssh date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:32:50:117474000
VM: 11:32:50:334508104
After: 11:32:50:291353000
```

Running on Mac in container using `run`(starting container takes too long):
```
$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman run --rm fedora date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:25:39:112380000
Container: 11:25:39:315971192
After: 11:25:39:324804000


$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman run --rm fedora date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:25:39:597259000
Container: 11:25:39:808665663
After: 11:25:39:831379000


$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman run --rm fedora date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:25:40:135964000
Container: 11:25:40:334109213
After: 11:25:40:350766000
```

Testing on Mac in running container using `exec`:
```
$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman exec -it keen_goldberg date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:30:54:078899000
Container: 11:30:54:245054968
After: 11:30:54:239056000


$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman exec -it keen_goldberg date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:31:00:062430000
Container: 11:31:00:209306858
After: 11:31:00:200675000


$ echo -n "Before: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "Container: " && podman exec -it keen_goldberg date -u +%H:%M:%S:%N && echo -n "After: " && gdate -u +%H:%M:%S:%N
Before: 11:31:00:811597000
Container: 11:31:00:973403346
After: 11:31:00:968653000
```

[2] 
```
[core@localhost ~]$ chronyc sources
```

> In conclusion, I think it would be a good idea to disable chronyd and install qemu-guest-agent in our Fedora CoreOS image, then add a small patch to Podman to create the Podman machine VM with the vfkit `--timesync` argument. I think it's more important to be in sync with the guest rather than NTP, even if the guest is slightly inaccurate ;)
> 
> Let me know what you think!

@ninja-quokka can you open an issue or PR (preferred) on containers/podman-machine-os ?

cc @Luap99 wdyt?

How would this works across the board, i.e. krunkit, hyperV and qemu on linux? I suppose we still need chrony for at least some of them to sync time properly?

As for podman-machine-os the image is used for all of them so if we need a subset it must use a option like `ConditionVirtualization=apple` in the service file, i.e. what we are doing with rosetta. 

That said I would recommend whatever the fix is you wait a week or so before proposing any changes to podman-machine-os. I do a rework there to include WSL as well so many things change and that will cause conflicts otherwise that I like to avoid.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@ninja-quokka care to follow up on this during bug week ?
Yep this is the specific task I want to tackle this bug week!
